story:
In his past life, Jiang WeiSheng had been a slave. Patience left his body long ago. Every muscle strained toward freedom, toward any door that might open. So when the first notes of flute and zither lifted into the night, he slipped from the meditation room despite his master’s sharp “Jiang Shen!”—the courtesy name his elders used. The music called him. He could not ignore it. Tonight, he told himself, he would see the dark shadow again.

He had first met that shadow the night he arrived at the palace with the Qi faction of the Huashan Sect. After a long journey, he walked back alone from the evening meal, humming the lullaby his mother once used to soothe him. A talisman flashed onto his robe. His limbs locked. A wisp of obsidian gathered around him, breath-stealing and close. Heat rose under his skin. Three heartbeats, and the thing was gone. His body was unchanged, but something in him did not recover.

Now the Empress would host a ceremony he and his fellow cultivators had prepared, a great net of spells to lure and seize a malicious spirit haunting the palace. Jiang was counting on the shadow to return.

He dressed, slipped into the great hall, and hid behind a huge celadon urn. Sandalwood and far-off spices threaded the air. His body, trained for balance and grace, longed to dance, but not tonight. He waited. The array chalked across the floor glowed pale in the lamplight.

A prickle lifted the hairs along his neck. A whisper brushed his ear: “Jiang Shen.”

He drew his sword. Its white sheen burst across the hall, revealing the array’s sigils and, within them, the shadow—not a smear of smoke this time, but a young man. He moved with quick, sure feet that blurred the demon-catching marks into harmless dust. He glanced up at Jiang and flashed a brilliant smile, then swept forward, one arm firm around Jiang’s waist. A leap, another, and they were in the cool night air.

For a trembling moment, Jiang forgot to struggle. The warmth of the stranger’s body startled him, the cries from the hall only distant threads—until they sharpened into screams.

“Stop! Let go! What have you done?”

They landed on a low rooftop over a quiet courtyard. The night was moonless; the young man’s half-smile and white teeth glinted faintly. He did not release Jiang until both were steady.

“You were fooled,” he said. “She meant to have you killed. Your sect’s array made a fine excuse. Your companions are likely already attacked. I chose another fate for you. Now we are here.”

Wind hushed through the pines. Jiang found no words. The stranger waited as if he had endless time. Finally, Jiang’s voice returned.

“Who are you?”

“Hua LiXuan.”

“The Empress’s assassin.”

“I am,” he said. “I was.”

They regarded each other in the dimness. Hua LiXuan stood perfectly still. Only his hair ribbon and the edges of his robe moved with the breeze. Jiang’s hand ached with the impulse to touch that ribbon.

“Where do I go now?” Jiang asked, quiet as the night.

“We,” Hua LiXuan said, as if correcting a small mistake. “I will follow you.”
